pattern: Because every walkable cell must be visited exactly once, exhaustively backtrack from the start, marking cells visited, and count completions that reach the end after covering all empty cells.
hints: The grid is tiny (at most 20 cells), so a full depth-first search that tries every direction is fast enough.
Count the empty cells first; a path is valid only if it ends on the target having stepped on exactly that many cells plus the start.

Backtrack from the start cell, marking each visited square as an obstacle temporarily and decrementing a remaining-count; when you land on the end with zero non-obstacle squares left, increment the answer, then unmark on the way back.

edge_inputs.description: grid with an obstacle, two valid paths
no path covers every empty cell
start adjacent to end, single cell between
obstacles split the grid so no full-coverage path exists
start and end with one empty cell to cover

stress.description: small random grid (<=16 cells) with one start, one end, and a few obstacles
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    total = max(2, min(16, size))
    # choose dimensions whose product is total-ish
    rows = rng.randint(1, 4)
    cols = max(2, (total // rows))
    cols = min(cols, 6)
    cells = rows * cols
    grid = [[0] * cols for _ in range(rows)]
    positions = [(r, c) for r in range(rows) for c in range(cols)]
    rng.shuffle(positions)
    sr, sc = positions[0]
    er, ec = positions[1]
    grid[sr][sc] = 1
    grid[er][ec] = 2
    # add a few obstacles among the remaining cells
    nobs = rng.randint(0, max(0, (cells - 2) // 3))
    for k in range(2, 2 + nobs):
        r, c = positions[k]
        grid[r][c] = -1
    return [grid]
stress.note: kept small because the search is exponential in empty cells
